title: Why Do Men Develop Parkinson’s Disease More Often Than Women?
gist: Research presented at a European neuroscience conference links Parkinson's higher prevalence in men to sex-specific gene-expression and DNA-methylation differences in brain cells, with women showing methylation changes in 69 genome regions versus just two in men.

insights.claim: Parkinson's disease is roughly twice as common in men as in women, a disparity researchers are working to explain.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A large, unexplained sex gap in a major neurodegenerative disease.
insights.locator: It's also around twice as common

insights.claim: Parkinson's develops from a combination of genetics, aging, and environmental factors such as pesticide exposure, rather than a single cause.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Multifactorial model shapes prevention and risk research.

insights.claim: In agricultural workers with early Parkinson's, women showed DNA methylation changes in 69 genome regions while men showed only two, pointing to sex-specific epigenetic mechanisms.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Striking quantitative sex difference in epigenetic markers.

insights.claim: Post-mortem brain analysis found Parkinson's caused similar overall stress changes in both sexes but differing genetic activity in key neural cells, astrocytes and oligodendrocytes.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Localizes the sex difference to specific glial cell types.
